Identification Mathematics: Recovering Latent Effects under Differential Misclassification

Davood Tofighi, Ph.D.

2026-06-22

Source: vignettes/articles/identification-math.qmd

Overview

medrobust derives partial-identification bounds for natural direct and indirect effects (NDE/NIE) when the mediator MM or the exposure AA is subject to differential misclassification — measurement error whose sensitivity/specificity depend on the outcome YY. This vignette documents the identification mathematics the package implements:

  1. the target estimand and its g-computation;
  2. mediator identification via two per-outcome 2×22\times2 systems;
  3. exposure identification via a closed-form 2×22\times2 inverse;
  4. the testable solvability condition Sny+Spy≠1Sn_y + Sp_y \neq 1.

It also records two correctness fixes made in version 0.1.0.9000 and the verification evidence behind them.

The estimand and g-computation

For binary AA, MM, YY with confounders CC, the natural effects compare three counterfactual outcome means E[Y(a,M(a′))]E[Y(a, M(a'))]. The mediator is binary, so the inner expectation over MM is exact, and we average over CC:

E[Y(a,M(a′))]=∑cP(C=c)∑m∈{0,1}P(M=m∣a′,c)P(Y=1∣a,m,c). E[Y(a, M(a'))] = \sum_{c} P(C=c) \sum_{m \in \{0,1\}} P(M=m \mid a', c)\, P(Y=1 \mid a, m, c).

This is the g-formula. The crucial point is that the outcome is averaged over the distributions of MM and CC — the expectation is taken after the nonlinear link, not before. Collapsing MM or CC to a mean and plugging it into expit(⋅)\mathrm{expit}(\cdot) introduces a Jensen-inequality bias.

Fix #1 — estimand (v0.1.0.9000)

The simulator’s ground-truth helper compute_true_effects() previously used a plug-in-mean-MM formula, biasing the reference NDE_OR to ≈1.500\approx 1.500 where the correct g-computation gives ≈1.480\approx 1.480. The simulator now Monte-Carlo g-computes over the empirical CC distribution, so the simulation “truth” matches the estimand the bounds target.

Differential misclassification

Let M*M^* be the observed (mismeasured) mediator. Differential error means the sensitivity and specificity depend on YY:

Sny=P(M*=1∣M=1,Y=y),Spy=P(M*=0∣M=0,Y=y),y∈{0,1}. Sn_y = P(M^*=1 \mid M=1, Y=y), \qquad Sp_y = P(M^*=0 \mid M=0, Y=y), \qquad y \in \{0,1\}.

Within a fixed exposure/confounder cell (a,c)(a, c) write π=P(M=1∣a,c)\pi = P(M=1\mid a,c), g1=P(Y=1∣M=1,a,c)g_1 = P(Y=1\mid M=1,a,c), g0=P(Y=1∣M=0,a,c)g_0 = P(Y=1\mid M=0,a,c). The four latent joint cells P(M=m,Y=y∣a,c)P(M=m, Y=y\mid a,c) are

P(M=1,Y=1)=πg1,P(M=0,Y=1)=(1−π)g0,P(M=1,Y=0)=π(1−g1),P(M=0,Y=0)=(1−π)(1−g0). \begin{aligned} P(M{=}1,Y{=}1) &= \pi g_1, & P(M{=}0,Y{=}1) &= (1-\pi) g_0,\\ P(M{=}1,Y{=}0) &= \pi (1-g_1), & P(M{=}0,Y{=}0) &= (1-\pi)(1-g_0). \end{aligned}

Mediator identification: two 2×22\times2 systems

Because the error rates differ by YY, the Y=1Y=1 and Y=0Y=0 observed cells form two independent 2×22\times2 systems. Writing x1=πg1x_1=\pi g_1, x0=(1−π)g0x_0=(1-\pi)g_0 (the Y=1Y=1 block) and z1=π(1−g1)z_1=\pi(1-g_1), z0=(1−π)(1−g0)z_0=(1-\pi)(1-g_0) (the Y=0Y=0 block):

(Sn11−Sp11−Sn1Sp1)⏟A1(x1x0)=(P11P10),(Sn01−Sp01−Sn0Sp0)⏟A0(z1z0)=(P01P00), \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} Sn_1 & 1-Sp_1\\ 1-Sn_1 & Sp_1\end{pmatrix}}_{A_1} \begin{pmatrix}x_1\\ x_0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}P_{11}\\ P_{10}\end{pmatrix}, \qquad \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} Sn_0 & 1-Sp_0\\ 1-Sn_0 & Sp_0\end{pmatrix}}_{A_0} \begin{pmatrix}z_1\\ z_0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}P_{01}\\ P_{00}\end{pmatrix},

where Pym*=P(Y=y,M*=m*∣a,c)P_{ym^*}=P(Y=y, M^*=m^*\mid a,c). Each block is solvable iff

det⁡(Ay)=Sny+Spy−1≠0. \det(A_y) = Sn_y + Sp_y - 1 \neq 0.

Recover the structural parameters by π=x1+z1\pi = x_1 + z_1, g1=x1/πg_1 = x_1/\pi, g0=x0/(1−π)g_0 = x_0/(1-\pi).

Fix #2 — mediator solve (v0.1.0.9000)

The previous implementation stacked these into a single 3×33\times3 system whose P01P_{01} row multiplied (1−Sp0)(1-Sp_0) by the Y=1Y=1 unknown x0=(1−π)g0x_0=(1-\pi)g_0, where the Y=0Y=0 equation actually involves (1−π)(1−g0)(1-\pi)(1-g_0). The system was internally inconsistent, biasing (π,γ)(\pi, \gamma) and offsetting the bounds (NDE overstated, NIE understated, by $$0.05–0.12 on the OR scale). Splitting into the two 2×22\times2 systems above resolves it. The bias was largest under strong differential error (Sn1≠Sn0Sn_1 \neq Sn_0), exactly where the mis-coupling bites hardest.

Exact-population recovery (runnable)

With population cells (no sampling) the solve is exact to machine precision — including a differential case:

true_parc <- function(a, cc, bM=-1.5, bY=-2.0, bAM=log(2.5),
                      tAY=log(1.5), tMY=log(2.5), bC=0.3, tC=0.3) {
  list(pi = expit(bM + bAM*a + bC*cc),
       g1 = expit(bY + tAY*a + tMY*1 + tC*cc),
       g0 = expit(bY + tAY*a + tMY*0 + tC*cc))
}
obs_pop <- function(a, cc, Sn1, Sp1, Sn0, Sp0) {
  p <- true_parc(a, cc)
  c(P11 = Sn1*p$pi*p$g1 + (1-Sp1)*(1-p$pi)*p$g0,
    P10 = (1-Sn1)*p$pi*p$g1 + Sp1*(1-p$pi)*p$g0,
    P01 = Sn0*p$pi*(1-p$g1) + (1-Sp0)*(1-p$pi)*(1-p$g0),
    P00 = (1-Sn0)*p$pi*(1-p$g1) + Sp0*(1-p$pi)*(1-p$g0))
}
solve_two_2x2 <- function(P, Sn1, Sp1, Sn0, Sp0) {
  xy1 <- solve(matrix(c(Sn1,1-Sp1,1-Sn1,Sp1), 2, 2, byrow=TRUE), c(P["P11"],P["P10"]))
  xy0 <- solve(matrix(c(Sn0,1-Sp0,1-Sn0,Sp0), 2, 2, byrow=TRUE), c(P["P01"],P["P00"]))
  pi_a <- xy1[1] + xy0[1]
  c(pi = unname(pi_a), g1 = unname(xy1[1]/pi_a), g0 = unname(xy1[2]/(1-pi_a)))
}

# Differential error: Sn1 != Sn0, Sp1 != Sp0
err <- 0
for (cc in 0:1) for (a in 0:1) {
  P  <- obs_pop(a, cc, Sn1=0.95, Sp1=0.85, Sn0=0.80, Sp0=0.92)
  rp <- solve_two_2x2(P, 0.95, 0.85, 0.80, 0.92)
  tp <- true_parc(a, cc)
  err <- max(err, abs(tp$pi-rp["pi"]), abs(tp$g1-rp["g1"]), abs(tp$g0-rp["g0"]))
}
cat(sprintf("max |recovered - true| (differential case) = %.2e\n", err))
max |recovered - true| (differential case) = 5.55e-17

Exposure identification: closed-form 2×22\times2 inverse

For exposure misclassification (A*A^* observed, error depending on YY), within a cell (m,y,c)(m, y, c) let p1=P(A=1∣m,y,c)p_1 = P(A=1\mid m,y,c), p0=1−p1p_0 = 1-p_1, and observed P1*=P(A*=1∣m,y,c)P^*_1 = P(A^*=1\mid m,y,c). The map is again a 2×22\times2 system,

P1*=Snyp1+(1−Spy)p0,P0*=(1−Sny)p1+Spyp0, P^*_1 = Sn_y\, p_1 + (1-Sp_y)\, p_0, \qquad P^*_0 = (1-Sn_y)\, p_1 + Sp_y\, p_0,

with det⁡=Sny+Spy−1\det = Sn_y + Sp_y - 1, so by Cramer’s rule

p1=SpyP1*−(1−Spy)P0*Sny+Spy−1,p0=SnyP0*−(1−Sny)P1*Sny+Spy−1. p_1 = \frac{Sp_y\,P^*_1 - (1-Sp_y)\,P^*_0}{Sn_y + Sp_y - 1}, \qquad p_0 = \frac{Sn_y\,P^*_0 - (1-Sn_y)\,P^*_1}{Sn_y + Sp_y - 1}.

This is exactly the closed form in bound_ne_exposure.R. It was independently audited and is correct (recovery verified to ∼10−16\sim10^{-16}); the exposure path never shared the mediator 3×33\times3 defect.

Convergence of the bound to the truth

With the solve fixed, bound_ne() evaluated at a degenerate region equal to the true Ψ=(Sn0=0.9,Sp0=0.9,ψsn=1,ψsp=1)\Psi=(Sn_0{=}0.9, Sp_0{=}0.9, \psi_{sn}{=}1, \psi_{sp}{=}1) converges to the oracle NDE/NIE as the sample size grows — confirming the remaining gap at moderate nn is finite-sample, not bias:

nn bound NDE_OR (midpoint) gap to oracle (1.48025)
5×1055\times10^{5} 1.49665 0.016
2×1062\times10^{6} 1.48055 0.0003
8×1068\times10^{6} 1.48110 0.0009 
# Reproduce (slow; not evaluated at build time):
reg <- sensitivity_region(c(0.899, 0.901), c(0.899, 0.901), c(1, 1), c(1, 1))
sim <- simulate_dm_data(
  n = 2e6,
  true_params = list(beta_AM = log(2.5), theta_AY = log(1.5), theta_MY = log(2.5)),
  dm_params = list(sn0 = 0.9, sp0 = 0.9, psi_sn = 1, psi_sp = 1),
  misclass_type = "mediator", confounders = 1, seed = 7)
b <- bound_ne(sim@observed, exposure = "A", mediator = "M_star", outcome = "Y",
              confounders = "C1", misclassified_variable = "mediator",
              sensitivity_region = reg, n_grid = 10, effect_scale = "OR")
c(NDE = (b@NDE_lower + b@NDE_upper) / 2, NIE = (b@NIE_lower + b@NIE_upper) / 2)

Finite-sample coverage and inference

The estimated endpoints L̂,Û\hat L, \hat U are consistent (population recovery is exact to 5×10−175\times10^{-17}, and at a fixed Ψ\Psi the effect estimate is essentially unbiased), but the raw estimated set [L̂,Û][\hat L, \hat U] is not a confidence set. At finite nn it under-covers the true effect, with coverage rising to nominal as n→∞n\to\infty (e.g. mediator NDE, ψ=1\psi=1: ≈0.20\approx 0.20 at n=500n{=}500, 0.830.83 at n=20,000n{=}20{,}000).

This is the Imbens–Manski problem, not estimator bias

A natural first guess is finite-sample bias of the endpoints. Diagnostics rule that out: at a fixed Ψ\Psi the effect estimate is unbiased, and the population bound contains the truth. The cause is geometric — the identified set is narrow (width ∼0.15\sim 0.15 for NDE here) relative to the sampling SD of the endpoints at small nn. When set-width ≈\approx endpoint-SD, a point estimate of the set under-covers the true parameter. This is exactly the setting of Imbens & Manski (2004), and the rise of coverage with nn is its signature (the SD shrinks relative to the fixed width).

The principled fix is a confidence interval for the set that widens the endpoints by their sampling uncertainty. The Imbens–Manski interval is [L̂−cSÊ(L̂),Û+cSÊ(Û)], \big[\, \hat L - c\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat L),\;\; \hat U + c\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat U)\,\big], where the critical value cc interpolates between the one-sided z1−αz_{1-\alpha} (when the set is wide relative to the SEs) and the two-sided z1−α/2z_{1-\alpha/2} (when the set is short). The endpoint SEs are obtained cheaply by re-evaluating the effect at the fixed argmin/argmax Ψ\Psi on resampled data (one evaluation per resample, no grid search — orders of magnitude faster than re-running the whole bootstrap). This restores nominal coverage at small nn:

effect raw-bound coverage (n=500n{=}500) Imbens–Manski CI coverage
NDE 0.090.09 0.950.95
NIE 0.120.12 0.930.93

This pattern holds across the full design: a simulation over both misclassification paths, N∈{100,200,500}N\in\{100,200,500\}, and the OR/RR/RD scales finds raw-bound coverage of 0.00–0.420.00\text{–}0.42 versus Imbens–Manski coverage of 0.90–1.000.90\text{–}1.00 (conservative at the smallest NN).

In the package this is bound_ci(), called on a fitted bound:

b  <- bound_ne(data, exposure = "A_star", mediator = "M", outcome = "Y",
               confounders = "C1", misclassified_variable = "exposure",
               sensitivity_region = region, n_grid = 50, effect_scale = "OR")
ci <- bound_ci(b, data, exposure = "A_star", mediator = "M", outcome = "Y",
               confounders = "C1", misclassified_variable = "exposure")
ci$NDE   # lower, upper (point bounds); ci_lower, ci_upper (Imbens-Manski CI)

Cost

A full nonparametric bootstrap re-runs the entire grid search per replicate and is prohibitive for coverage studies. The endpoint-SE construction above evaluates only at the two optimal Ψ\Psi, so it is feasible at scale; a closed-form delta-method SE is a further optimization.

Practical implication: grid resolution

Because identification is exact only in the population, the partial-identification bound covers the truth reliably only on a sufficiently dense grid over the sensitivity region. For simulation or applied work, use n_grid ≳50\gtrsim 50 when the region is wide; coarse grids can miss the parameter combination that brackets the true effect.

References

  • Tofighi, D. (2025). Partial identification under differential misclassification. (In preparation.)
  • Manski, C. F. (2003). Partial Identification of Probability Distributions.
  • VanderWeele, T. J. (2015). Explanation in Causal Inference.
